figures.caption: Table 4: INDEXED versus DEEP-INDEXED across three content types. Median page reads (R̃) and accuracy. Hash and numeric content: retrieval works as predicted. Encyclopedia content: DEEP-INDEXED accuracy collapses at M ≥ 200 despite median reads near 1, indicating the model generates answers from parametric memory rather than reading them from pages. The median reads of 0.0 at M = 200 means the majority of trials hit the token budget before reading any page. 30 trials per cell except hash M ≥ 1,000 (50 trials) and hash M ≥ 3,000 (20 trials).

Figure 4: Multi-model comparison. Left: GPT-4o-mini cannot sustain binary search on sorted pages; FLAT-SORTED converges to FLAT at M = 500. Center: GPT-5.4 binary-searches effectively (R̃ = 5 at M = 500), but INDEXED and DEEP-INDEXED hold at 1. Right: accuracy by condition and model. Solid lines: GPT-4o-mini. Dashed: GPT-5.4.

Table 3: Multi-model comparison on hash content (30 trials/cell). SORT = FLAT-SORTED, IDX = INDEXED, DEEP = DEEP-INDEXED. GPT-5.4 achieves near-optimal binary search on sorted pages (5 reads at M = 500 vs. theoretical log2 50 ≈ 5.6) but INDEXED still dominates at 1 read.

Figure 1: Page reads (R) and accuracy versus item count M for the three conditions (hash content). FLAT (red) grows linearly, tracking the M/(2P ) prediction. INDEXED (blue) remains constant at ≈1. INDEXED-CORRUPTED (pink) exceeds FLAT at large M , confirming causal index use. Shaded bands show interquartile range.

Figure 5: Two-level indexing: median page reads, tokens, and accuracy across store sizes. INDEXED (blue) degrades at M ≥ 1,000; DEEP-INDEXED (green) holds near 1–2. FLAT (red, dashed) shown for reference. Shaded bands show interquartile range.

Figure 6: Content familiarity disrupts retrieval. DEEP-INDEXED performance across three content types at matched store sizes. Left: page reads—hash and numeric hold at 1; encyclopedia collapses. Center: token cost—encyclopedia inflates by 50× as trials exhaust the 100K budget. Right: accuracy—encyclopedia drops to 27% at M = 200. Shaded bands show interquartile range.

Figure 2: Left: separation ratio (FLAT/INDEXED page reads) versus M , with theoretical prediction M/20 (dashed). Right: per-trial read distributions by condition at each M . FLAT broadens rightward; INDEXED collapses to 1.

Figure 3: Token cost (Tok) versus item count M (hash content). Left: median total tokens per trial by condition (shaded bands show interquartile range). FLAT scales as ≈M2; INDEXED is orders of magnitude cheaper. Right: token cost ratio FLAT/INDEXED (median).
